params.label: Background
params.notes: We open with the biology so the modeling lands later. Three ideas to plant here: CRISPR is a 20-letter guided cut, real scientists SCORE a guide from its sequence instead of testing every one, and the seed region next to the PAM matters most. Transition: let's start with what a guide actually is.

params.code:
img_h = body_h - 1.05
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g1_crispr/slides/figures/intro_guide_anatomy.png', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.05, max_w=body_w, max_h=img_h)
_add_text(slide, 'The 20 letters in our dataset ARE this guide sequence. Cas9 locks onto the PAM (the NGG landmark), then cuts a few letters upstream.',
          left=body_l + 1.0, top=body_top + img_h + 0.18, width=body_w - 2.0, height=0.62,
          size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, italic=True, align=PP_ALIGN.CENTER)
params.lede: CRISPR-Cas9 is a pair of molecular scissors you aim at one spot in DNA using a short 20-letter guide (the letters are A, C, G, T). Where the guide matches, the scissors cut -- and that cut turns a gene off or repairs a mutation.
params.notes:
Open concrete and physical. CRISPR-Cas9 is scissors plus a 20-letter address label. Point at the
figure: the turquoise block is the guide/protospacer, the pink block just to its right is the PAM
(always NGG), and Cas9 snips a few letters upstream. Every guide in our dataset is one of these
20-letter strings. Do not mention machine learning yet -- just make sure they can read the picture.
Transition: so how do real scientists decide which 20 letters to use?
params.title: What CRISPR actually is

params.code:
cards = [
    ('SCORE, DO NOT EYEBALL', 'The go-to tool is Doench 2016 Rule Set 2 / Azimuth, which scores a guide straight from its sequence [1], built on the earlier position-and-PAM rules of Rule Set 1 [2].', TURQUOISE_RGB),
    ('THE SEED IS KING', 'Cas9 grabs a landmark called the PAM, then checks the handful of letters right next to it -- the seed. Guides that mismatch there usually fail to cut [3][4].', DEEPPINK_RGB),
    ('NEURAL NETS PUSH FURTHER', 'Newer tools like DeepCRISPR learn the same job with a neural network and far more data, beating hand-built rules [5][6].', BLUEVIOLET_RGB),
]
gap = 0.35
cw = (body_w - gap * 2) / 3
ch = body_h - 0.15
block_h = 1.9
by = body_top + (ch - block_h) / 2
bh = (body_top + ch) - (by + 0.58) - 0.30
for i, (lab, bod, col) in enumerate(cards):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + gap)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x + 0.05, top=body_top + 0.06, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=_rgb('#E4E1D9'))
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=WHITE_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.08, fill_rgb=col)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.28, top=by, width=cw - 0.56, height=0.45,
              size=15, color_rgb=col, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=x + 0.28, top=by + 0.58, width=cw - 0.56, height=bh,
              size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: Two guides that look almost identical can behave completely differently -- one cutting beautifully, the other barely at all. Testing each one in the lab takes weeks, so scientists predict cutting efficiency from the 20 letters instead of measuring it.
params.notes:
This is the professional context. Real guide selection is a scoring problem, not a guessing game.
Name-drop the three ideas the way the field actually thinks about them: (1) the Doench Rule Set 2 /
Azimuth model scores sequence directly [1][2]; (2) the seed region near the PAM dominates [3][4]; and
(3) modern neural nets like DeepCRISPR push accuracy further with more data [5][6]. Students should
leave knowing the seed idea. Transition: let's look at that seed effect as a picture.
params.title: How real scientists pick a guide

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.60
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g1_crispr/slides/figures/intro_seed_importance.png', left=body_l, top=body_top, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h)
ax = body_l + fw + 0.35
aw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_text(slide, 'READ THE SHAPE', left=ax, top=body_top + 0.10, width=aw, height=0.4,
          size=16, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, 'The bars stay low across the far end of the guide, then rise sharply toward the PAM. That rising block is the seed, or core region.',
          left=ax, top=body_top + 0.62, width=aw, height=1.7, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
_add_text(slide, 'Keep this picture in mind. With no biology lessons at all, our own model is about to rediscover this exact shape.',
          left=ax, top=body_top + 2.45, width=aw, height=1.9, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: Not all 20 letters count the same. The letters nearest the PAM -- the seed, roughly positions 13 to 20 -- carry far more of the signal about whether a guide will cut.
params.notes:
Plant the single most important intuition: the 20 letters are not equal. The chart rises toward the
PAM end -- that block is the seed. This is a redraw of the Zheng 2017 / Doench 2016 finding [4][1].
Tell them to remember the shape, because the payoff slide later shows OUR model reproducing it from
scratch. Transition: now to our actual project -- the task and the data.
params.title: The seed region matters most

params.label: Methods
params.notes: Now we turn that biology into a machine-learning task. Coming up: the exact dataset, the class imbalance, and the single decision that dominates everything -- how we turn DNA letters into numbers. Keep the seed idea handy, because it drives the prediction we are about to make.

params.code:
tiles = [
    ('5,310', 'real 20-letter guides, each with a measured cutting score from 0 to 1', DEEPPINK_RGB),
    ('17', 'different human genes, so the model must learn sequence rules, not memorize one gene', BLUEVIOLET_RGB),
    ('~20%', 'of guides cut well, so a lazy "always low" guesser looks 80 percent accurate and is useless', TURQUOISE_RGB),
]
gap = 0.35
cw = (body_w - gap * 2) / 3
ch = body_h - 0.10
num_h = 1.55
lab_h = 1.75
block_top = body_top + (ch - (num_h + 0.15 + lab_h)) / 2
for i, (num, lab, col) in enumerate(tiles):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + gap)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=col)
    tc = _text_on(col)
    _add_text(slide, num, left=x + 0.28, top=block_top, width=cw - 0.56, height=num_h,
              size=62, color_rgb=tc, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.28, top=block_top + num_h + 0.15, width=cw - 0.56, height=lab_h,
              size=16, color_rgb=tc, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: We use the exact dataset behind the famous Rule Set 2 model [1]: real guides that scientists actually measured in the lab. Our job is to predict, from the 20 letters alone, whether a guide is a strong cutter.
params.notes:
Set up the task in numbers. 5,310 real, lab-measured guides across 17 genes -- the same data behind
the Rule Set 2 model [1]. The number to dwell on is the last one: only about 1 in 5 guides is a good
cutter. That class imbalance is why, two slides from now, we refuse to trust plain accuracy. Say it
out loud: an 'always low' model would be 80 percent accurate and completely useless. Transition: the
first real decision -- how do we hand DNA letters to a model?
params.title: The question we are asking

params.code:
gap = 0.35
strip_h = 1.55
inner_gap = 0.40
card_h = body_h - strip_h - inner_gap - 0.15
cw = (body_w - gap) / 2
cards = [
    ('ONE-HOT  (keeps the order)', 'Four on/off switches per position, so the model always knows which letter sits where. That is 20 positions times 4 = 80 numbers per guide.', TURQUOISE_RGB),
    ('K-MER  (ignores the order)', 'Just counts how many of each letter and letter-pair appear, throwing the position away. Only 21 numbers per guide.', AMBER_RGB),
]
block_h = 1.35
by = body_top + (card_h - block_h) / 2
bh = (body_top + card_h) - (by + 0.60) - 0.25
for i, (lab, bod, col) in enumerate(cards):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + gap)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x + 0.05, top=body_top + 0.06, width=cw, height=card_h, fill_rgb=_rgb('#E4E1D9'))
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=body_top, width=cw, height=card_h, fill_rgb=WHITE_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.08, fill_rgb=col)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.28, top=by, width=cw - 0.56, height=0.45,
              size=16, color_rgb=col, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=x + 0.28, top=by + 0.60, width=cw - 0.56, height=bh,
              size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
sy = body_top + card_h + inner_gap
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=sy, width=body_w, height=strip_h, fill_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB)
tc = _text_on(DEEPPINK_RGB)
_add_text(slide, 'LISTEN   vs   SILENT', left=body_l + 0.32, top=sy + 0.24, width=body_w - 0.64, height=0.5,
          size=24, color_rgb=tc, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, 'Same letters, different words. k-mer cannot tell them apart, but one-hot can -- and since biology cares exactly WHERE a letter sits (the seed!), one-hot should win.',
          left=body_l + 0.32, top=sy + 0.88, width=body_w - 0.64, height=0.55, size=15, color_rgb=tc, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: A computer cannot do math on the letter A, so we turn each guide into numbers -- and how we do that is the single most important choice in the whole project. We tried two ways and let the results decide.
params.notes:
The conceptual heart of the project. Featurizing = turning letters into numbers, and the choice
encodes what you think matters. one-hot keeps position (80 numbers); k-mer keeps only counts (21
numbers) and throws order away. The LISTEN/SILENT analogy is the whole lesson in two words: same
letters, different meaning -- order matters. Because the seed is a POSITION effect, we predict one-hot
wins. Make them commit to that prediction before the next slide. Transition: let's test it.
params.title: Turning letters into numbers

params.label: Results
params.notes: Time for what we found. Three beats here: keeping the letter order (one-hot) wins, why we grade with AUC instead of accuracy, and the payoff -- the model rediscovering the seed on its own. Then a harder task, predicting the exact cutting score.

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.62
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g1_crispr/slides/figures/representation_auc.png', left=body_l, top=body_top, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h)
ax = body_l + fw + 0.35
aw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_text(slide, 'THE REAL LESSON', left=ax, top=body_top + 0.10, width=aw, height=0.4,
          size=16, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, 'The one-hot bars (turquoise) top the k-mer bars (gold) for every one of the four models. How you prepared the data mattered more than which fancy model you picked.',
          left=ax, top=body_top + 0.62, width=aw, height=2.0, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
_add_text(slide, '0.77', left=ax, top=body_top + 2.75, width=aw, height=1.0,
          size=54, color_rgb=BLUEVIOLET_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, 'best AUC -- Random Forest on one-hot', left=ax, top=body_top + 3.75, width=aw, height=0.5,
          size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: We trained the same four models on each way of making numbers and compared them. Every single model did better with one-hot than with k-mer.
params.notes:
First result, and the transferable one. Same four models, two featurizations; one-hot wins across the
board. The takeaway is bigger than CRISPR: data representation beat model choice. The prediction they
made last slide came true, and for the right reason -- one-hot kept the position information where the
signal lives. Best score is Random Forest on one-hot at AUC 0.77. Transition: wait, why AUC and not
just accuracy?
params.title: Keeping the letter order wins

params.code:
gap = 0.40
cw = (body_w - gap) / 2
ch = body_h - 0.10
num_h = 1.7
txt_h = 1.9
block_top = body_top + (ch - (num_h + 0.15 + txt_h)) / 2
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB)
tc = _text_on(DEEPPINK_RGB)
_add_text(slide, '80%', left=body_l + 0.30, top=block_top, width=cw - 0.6, height=num_h,
          size=76, color_rgb=tc, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
_add_text(slide, 'accuracy of a lazy model that ALWAYS guesses "low" -- and never finds a single good guide. Accuracy is fooled by the imbalance.',
          left=body_l + 0.30, top=block_top + num_h + 0.15, width=cw - 0.6, height=txt_h, size=17, color_rgb=tc, font=SANS_FONT)
x2 = body_l + cw + gap
_add_rect(slide, left=x2, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=BLUEVIOLET_RGB)
tc2 = _text_on(BLUEVIOLET_RGB)
_add_text(slide, '0.77', left=x2 + 0.30, top=block_top, width=cw - 0.6, height=num_h,
          size=76, color_rgb=tc2, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, anchor=MSO_ANCHOR.MIDDLE)
_add_text(slide, 'AUC of our best model. AUC asks a fairer question: does it rank GOOD guides above bad ones? 0.5 is a coin flip, 1.0 is perfect.',
          left=x2 + 0.30, top=block_top + num_h + 0.15, width=cw - 0.6, height=txt_h, size=17, color_rgb=tc2, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: Because only about 20 percent of guides cut well, plain accuracy is easy to fake. AUC asks a fairer question.
params.notes:
The judgment slide. On imbalanced data accuracy lies: an 'always low' model scores 80 percent and is
worthless because it never flags a usable guide. AUC sidesteps the trick -- it measures whether the
model RANKS good guides above bad ones, so 0.5 is a coin flip and 1.0 is perfect. Our 0.77 means real
ranking skill. This same trap (accuracy on imbalanced data) shows up everywhere in medical AI, so it
is worth over-teaching. Transition: now the most satisfying result.
params.title: Why we grade with AUC, not accuracy

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.62
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g1_crispr/slides/figures/position_importance.png', left=body_l, top=body_top, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h)
ax = body_l + fw + 0.35
aw = body_w - fw - 0.35
_add_text(slide, 'WHAT IT FOUND', left=ax, top=body_top + 0.10, width=aw, height=0.4,
          size=16, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, 'Its importance climbs toward the right end of the guide and peaks at position 20, right next to the PAM. That is the seed region -- exactly where the biology said the action is.',
          left=ax, top=body_top + 0.62, width=aw, height=2.2, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
_add_text(slide, 'The model learned real CRISPR biology from nothing but sequences and cut-scores. A model that agrees with known science is one people will trust.',
          left=ax, top=body_top + 2.85, width=aw, height=2.0, size=15, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: Now the payoff. We asked a simple, explainable model which positions along the guide it leaned on. Nobody ever told it about seeds.
params.notes:
The wow moment -- put it up and pause. We asked a plain logistic-regression model which of the 20
positions it relied on, and the importance peaks at position 20, right at the PAM: the seed. Flip back
to the background seed chart -- same shape. Nobody encoded that; the model recovered decades of CRISPR
biology from sequences and cut-scores alone. Tie it to trust: an explainable model that matches known
science is one clinicians and biologists will believe. Transition: can it predict the exact score,
not just high vs low?
params.title: The model rediscovered the seed

params.code:
fw = body_w * 0.54
_fit_image(slide, '/home/jiwei/outset-ai-healthcare/solutions/g1_crispr/slides/figures/regression_fit.png', left=body_l, top=body_top, max_w=fw, max_h=body_h)
ax = body_l + fw + 0.40
aw = body_w - fw - 0.40
_add_text(slide, '0.36', left=ax, top=body_top + 0.05, width=aw, height=0.95,
          size=52, color_rgb=BLUEVIOLET_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, 'R-squared: the share of the ups-and-downs the model explained (0 = guessing the average, 1 = perfect).',
          left=ax, top=body_top + 1.05, width=aw, height=1.0, size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
_add_text(slide, '0.61', left=ax, top=body_top + 2.15, width=aw, height=0.95,
          size=52, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, 'correlation between predicted and true efficiency.',
          left=ax, top=body_top + 3.15, width=aw, height=0.7, size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
_add_text(slide, 'Real signal, but far from perfect -- and that is honest. The pro tools scatter the same way [1][5][6].',
          left=ax, top=body_top + 3.95, width=aw, height=1.0, size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, italic=True)
params.lede: High versus low throws away detail, so we also tried to predict the exact 0-to-1 cutting score. R-squared says how much of the real variation we explained.
params.notes:
Second, harder task: predict the exact 0-to-1 score, not just a yes/no. Explain R-squared plainly --
the fraction of the real variation the model captures, 0 is guessing the mean, 1 is perfect. We get
0.36 (correlation 0.61): a real but loose trend, visible as a tilted cloud around the diagonal. The
honest point: the professional predicted-vs-measured plots scatter just like ours [1][5][6]. Useful
does not mean perfect. Transition: so let's be clear-eyed about what this toy can and cannot do.
params.title: Predicting the exact score

params.label: Being honest
params.notes: Good science states its own limits out loud. We lay out what this toy model genuinely shows, what it cannot do, and the off-target-safety question it says nothing about. Then the references and the one-sentence takeaway. This is the section the rubric rewards most.

params.code:
cards = [
    ('IT CAN', "Show that a guide's sequence really carries signal, and that the seed region matters -- on the very same data the professionals use [1].", TURQUOISE_RGB),
    ('IT CANNOT MATCH THE PROS', 'It will not beat a production tool, and it ignores the cell-type and chromatin effects that change cutting inside a living cell [7].', DEEPPINK_RGB),
    ('IT CANNOT JUDGE SAFETY', 'It says nothing about off-target risk -- whether the guide also cuts the wrong places elsewhere in the genome [8].', BLUEVIOLET_RGB),
]
gap = 0.35
cw = (body_w - gap * 2) / 3
ch = body_h - 0.15
block_h = 1.7
by = body_top + (ch - block_h) / 2
bh = (body_top + ch) - (by + 0.58) - 0.30
for i, (lab, bod, col) in enumerate(cards):
    x = body_l + i * (cw + gap)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x + 0.05, top=body_top + 0.06, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=_rgb('#E4E1D9'))
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=body_top, width=cw, height=ch, fill_rgb=WHITE_RGB)
    _add_rect(slide, left=x, top=body_top, width=cw, height=0.08, fill_rgb=col)
    _add_text(slide, lab, left=x + 0.28, top=by, width=cw - 0.56, height=0.45,
              size=15, color_rgb=col, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
    _add_text(slide, bod, left=x + 0.28, top=by + 0.58, width=cw - 0.56, height=bh,
              size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: A good project names its own limits out loud. Ours is a demonstration of an idea, not a design tool -- and here is exactly where the line sits.
params.notes:
Model the intellectual honesty the rubric rewards. Green light: it genuinely shows sequence carries
signal and the seed matters, on the pros' own data [1]. Red lights: it will not beat a production tool
and cannot see cell-type or chromatin effects [7]; and crucially it says NOTHING about off-target
safety [8] -- the single most important question for real CRISPR use. State plainly: this builds
intuition, it does not choose a clinical guide. Transition: the papers, then the bottom line.
params.title: What this toy model can and cannot do

params.code:
colw = (body_w - 0.6) / 2
left_refs = ('[1] Doench et al. 2016, Nat Biotechnol -- Rule Set 2 / Azimuth; source of our dataset.\n\n'
             '[2] Doench et al. 2014, Nat Biotechnol -- Rule Set 1; position + PAM rules.\n\n'
             '[3] Hsu, Lander & Zhang 2014, Cell -- landmark plain-English CRISPR review.\n\n'
             '[4] Zheng et al. 2017, Sci Rep -- direct evidence the seed / core matters most.')
right_refs = ('[5] Chuai et al. 2018, Genome Biol -- DeepCRISPR, a CNN guide-efficiency model.\n\n'
              '[6] Kim et al. 2019, Sci Adv -- DeepSpCas9, trained on 12,832 sites.\n\n'
              '[7] Konstantakos et al. 2022, NAR -- survey comparing guide predictors.\n\n'
              '[8] Abbaszadeh & Shahlai 2025, arXiv -- explainable AI + off-target safety.')
_add_text(slide, 'FOUNDATIONS', left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.05, width=colw, height=0.4,
          size=16, color_rgb=BLUEVIOLET_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, left_refs, left=body_l, top=body_top + 0.6, width=colw, height=body_h - 0.7,
          size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
rx = body_l + colw + 0.6
_add_text(slide, 'MODERN MODELS & REVIEWS', left=rx, top=body_top + 0.05, width=colw, height=0.4,
          size=16, color_rgb=DEEPPINK_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True)
_add_text(slide, right_refs, left=rx, top=body_top + 0.6, width=colw, height=body_h - 0.7,
          size=14, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT)
params.lede: The eight papers behind this project -- from the plain-English CRISPR reviews to the models we measured ourselves against.
params.notes:
Give credit and show the trail. Left column is the foundation: the two Doench Rule Sets [1][2], the
Hsu/Lander/Zhang review [3], and the Zheng seed paper [4]. Right column is the modern neural-net line
and the surveys [5][6][7][8]. Do not read every line -- point out that [1] gave us the data, [4] is
the seed evidence our model reproduced, and [8] is the off-target-safety gap we cannot address.
Transition: the one sentence to remember.
params.title: References

params.code:
q_top = body_top + body_h * 0.28
_add_text(slide, 'A model you can explain is a model people trust.',
          left=body_l, top=q_top, width=body_w, height=1.1,
          size=32, color_rgb=BLUEVIOLET_RGB, font=MONO_FONT, bold=True, align=PP_ALIGN.CENTER)
rule_w = 2.4
_add_rect(slide, left=body_l + (body_w - rule_w) / 2, top=q_top + 1.35, width=rule_w, height=0.05, fill_rgb=BLUEVIOLET_RGB)
_add_text(slide, 'Use this project to build intuition -- then use a validated tool and the lab to actually choose a guide.',
          left=body_l + 1.0, top=q_top + 1.90, width=body_w - 2.0, height=1.0,
          size=19, color_rgb=INK_RGB, font=SANS_FONT, align=PP_ALIGN.CENTER)
params.notes:
Land the whole deck on one idea: explainability builds trust. Our toy model earned a little trust by
agreeing with known biology and by being honest about R-squared 0.36 and about off-target safety.
Leave them with the working rule for all of medical AI: a simple model you can explain and honestly
evaluate beats a fancy black box nobody can check. Then use the real tools and the lab to actually
pick a guide. Thank them.
params.title: The honest bottom line
